title: Track Your Work Hours
summary: Record the hours you work each shift accurately so your paycheck is correct.
learnerPromise: You will be able to record your work hours accurately, compare them to your pay stub, and spot errors before they become problems.
whyItMatters: Mistakes in payroll happen more often than people realize. Tracking your own hours helps you make sure you are paid fairly for the time you worked.
realLifeUses: Writing down your clock-in and clock-out times in a notebook or notes app each day
Comparing your tracked hours to your paycheck or pay stub to check for errors
Recording overtime or extra hours that might be missed by an electronic system
Keeping a record in case of a payroll dispute or missing payment
materialsNeeded: Your pay stub or pay record (to compare later)
result: A written or digital record of your work hours for at least one week, with dates, start times, and end times.
youWillLearn: How to record your start and end times for every shift in a consistent format
How to note breaks, overtime, or extra hours separately from regular hours
How to add up your total hours for a pay period
How to compare your tracked hours to your pay stub and what to do if they do not match
miniChallenge: Create a simple hours-tracking sheet and record your work hours — real or imaginary — for one week. Add up the total and compare it to a sample pay stub.
steps: Choose a tracking method: a small notebook, a notes app on your phone, or a simple spreadsheet.
For each workday, write the date, the time you started working, and the time you stopped.
Note any unpaid breaks separately and subtract them from your total if your workplace does not pay for break time.
At the end of each week, add up your total hours worked. Keep a running total for the pay period.
When you receive your pay stub, compare the hours listed to your own records. If they do not match, politely raise the issue with your supervisor or payroll department.
completionCriteria: You have a complete record of hours worked for at least one week, with dates and times
You can accurately add up your total hours and explain how to compare them to a pay stub
commonProblems: Forgetting to record times right away — do it immediately when you start and end your shift
Not accounting for unpaid breaks, which can add up over a week
Losing track of the notebook or file — keep it somewhere you access regularly
Not speaking up about a discrepancy because it feels uncomfortable — small errors add up over time
tips: Set a phone reminder to log your hours at the end of each shift if you tend to forget
Keep your records for at least a few months in case a question comes up later
If your workplace uses a time-clock app, screenshot your recorded hours each week as backup
Approach payroll questions as requests for clarification, not accusations — most discrepancies are honest mistakes
